request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"38-studios::gaming","name":"38 Studios","one_liner":"Celebrity-founded US game studio that built an MMO and a single-player RPG on a single $75 M government loan, collapsing in 2012 when sales fell short of the break-even threshold needed to fund the next milestone.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"38 Studios sold games as one-time purchases (Kingdoms of Amalur: Reckoning at retail). The new pitch is free-to-play monetized through cosmetics and a battle pass — a recurring, micro-transaction-driven model that is almost the inverse of a premium packaged title. The only overlap is that both target consumers in the gaming sector."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both compete in the consumer gaming market, and 38 Studios did pursue mobile/cross-platform play (Rise of Nations: Tactics on iOS/Unity). Both are aiming at genre-savvy players, but 38 Studios targeted MMORPG and single-player RPG audiences while the new pitch targets 4X strategy players on mobile and PC via Steam — overlapping but distinct sub-audiences."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on retail distribution through EA Partners and government-backed financing for a large-scale AAA production. The new pitch uses a free-to-play, platform-native distribution (App Store, Google Play, Steam) with no publisher partner or government loan — a fundamentally different go-to-market. Both do target broad consumer bases rather than enterprise clients."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios burned over $118 M in operating expenses before shipping a single title and had ~400 employees. The new pitch appears to be an early-stage indie/studio effort shipping a single F2P title across three platforms — far leaner. Both are pre-profitability game studios, but the scale and burn rate differ enormously."}},"why_similar":"Both are consumer-facing video game studios targeting strategy/fantasy-adjacent genres. Both rely on a single flagship title as the primary commercial vehicle. 38 Studios also explored mobile and cross-platform strategy (Rise of Nations: Tactics on iOS) and built a rich IP world as the foundation for a larger franchise — mirroring the new pitch's ambition to build a 4X property. Both face the core challenge of acquiring and retaining a loyal player base in a crowded gaming market.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization model: 38 Studios used one-time premium retail purchase; the new pitch uses free-to-play with cosmetics and a battle pass — a fundamentally different revenue and retention architecture. 2. Platform focus: 38 Studios targeted PC/console retail; the new pitch leads on iOS and Android with Steam as a secondary platform. 3. Scope and burn rate: 38 Studios employed ~400 people and spent >$118 M before its first release; the new pitch appears to be a significantly leaner operation. 4. Funding source: 38 Studios depended on a single $75 M government loan and Schilling's personal fortune; the new pitch has no indicated government dependency. 5. Genre: 38 Studios built an MMORPG and single-player RPG; the new pitch is a 4X strategy title — a different design and player demographic.","failure_causes":["single-title revenue dependency (Reckoning sales fell short of 2 M copy break-even)","over-reliance on a single government loan ($75 M) with hard repayment deadlines","excessive headcount and burn rate (~400 staff, >$118 M spent before profitability)","flagship MMO (Project Copernicus) never shipped, eliminating the primary revenue plan","founder's lack of business experience and internal executive dysfunction","collapsing MMO market made selling or licensing the core IP impossible","political and media backlash on government loan accelerated final collapse"],"lessons_for_input":["Never tie your studio's survival to hitting a single sales threshold — model multiple revenue scenarios and maintain a runway that survives a miss.","Free-to-play battle-pass monetization requires continuous live-ops investment; budget for ongoing content cadence from day one, not just launch.","Avoid concentrating all funding in one source; diversify across publisher advances, platform deals, and investor equity so no single creditor can force bankruptcy.","Ship a minimum viable version of your title early to validate player acquisition costs and battle-pass conversion rates before scaling the team.","Keep headcount lean until live-game metrics (DAU, ARPU, retention D1/D7/D30) prove the monetization loop works at a small scale."]}
